notes.note: The Quinquatrus, a five-day festival of Minerva in mid-March, was the occasion of the tribunes' incendiary contiones (public meetings) that Cicero had been hearing about. The festival period was notorious as a time for political provocations.

notes.anchor: lest I come back a plain stranger
notes.note: Cicero, still commanding his province, is anxious to re-enter Roman political life well-briefed; the phrase catches his mixed feelings — relief at leaving, fear of irrelevance on return.

Pessinus was the great Galatian cult-centre of Cybele (the Great Mother) in the interior of Asia Minor, roughly en route from Cilicia toward the Galatian client-kingdoms; Diogenes and Philo were heading there and then on to the tetrarch Adiatorix.

notes.anchor: The City, my dear Rufus, the City: hold to her
notes.note: Cicero's most famous formulation of his own regret at provincial exile, echoing his earlier letters to Atticus from exile (58 BC); 'Rufus' here is a literary address to Caelius whose cognomen was Rufus.

A triumph required the general to remain outside the city pomerium until the Senate voted it; Cicero had been acclaimed imperator after the Amanus campaign of 51 BC and held a lingering hope for the honour, though he would never receive it.
